в•‘в—Ңв—Ң[в—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹ]в—Ңв—Ң[вҷҘвҷҘвҷҘвҷҘ][вҷҘвҷҘвҷҘвҷҘ]в—Ңв—Ң[в—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹ]в—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв•‘ 2
в•‘в—Ңв—Ң[в—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹ]в—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ң[в—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹ][в—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹ]в—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ң[вҷ вҷ вҷ ]в—Ңв—Ңв•‘ 3

в•‘в—Ңв—Ң[в—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹв—Ҹ]в—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ң[в–Ів–Ів–Ів–Ів–І][в–Ів–Ів–Ів–Ів–І]в—Ңв—Ң[вҷЈвҷЈвҷЈ][вҷЈвҷЈвҷЈ]в—Ң[вҷ вҷ вҷ ]в—Ңв—Ңв•‘ 5
в•‘в—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ң[вҷ вҷ вҷ ]в—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ң[вҷ вҷ вҷ ]в—Ңв—Ңв•‘ 6
в•‘в—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ң[вҷ вҷ вҷ ][вҷ вҷ вҷ ]в—Ңв—ҢTв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ң[вҳҫвҳҫвҳҫвҳҫвҳҫвҳҫ]в—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв•‘ 7

в•‘в—ҢN[вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…][вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…вҳ…]в—Ңв—ҢXв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ң[вҷҘвҷҘвҷҘвҷҘ]в—Ңв—Ңв•‘ 9
в•‘в—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—ҢAв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—Ңв—ҢYв—Ңв—Ңв•‘ 10
в•ҡв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•қ

```

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?


Please send a message to the other player to start the game!<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

в•ҡв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•қ

```

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.

The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.

The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?

* The grid consists of an enclosing frame, empty spots marked by 'в—Ң', and background objects represented by blocks of symbols in square brackets ('[]').

* The upper edge displays x-coordinates increasing to the right with a step size of 5, and the right edge y-coordinates increasing downward.
* The following objects are randomly placed on your grid: 'S', 'Y', 'N', 'T', 'A', 'X'.


The other player sees a variation of the game board, where the objects are placed at different random locations. You cannot see the other player's board, and they cannot see yours.


**Goal:**

Both players need to move the objects on their respective background so that identical objects end up at the same coordinates. You have to communicate with the other player to agree upon a common goal configuration.


**Rules:**

* In each turn, you can send exactly one of the following two commands:
 1. `SAY: <MESSAGE>`: to send a message (everything up until the next line break) to the other player. I will forward it to your partner. Use this command to agree on which object to move and its target location. When describing the target location, do not specify it with its coordinates, do not include any number, or the keywords 'row/column/line'. Instead, use background objects as reference points.
2. `MOVE: <OBJECT>, (<X>, <Y>)`: to move an object to a new position, where `<X>` is the column and `<Y>` is the row. I will inform you if your move was successful or not.
* You must not use coordinates, digits, or one of the keywords 'row/column/line' when communicating with the other player.
* If you don't stick to the format, send several commands at once, or use forbidden content in a message, I have to penalize you.
* If both players accumulate more than 14 penalties, you both lose the game.
* It is vital that you communicate with the other player regarding your goal state! The *only* way you can transmit your strategy to the other player is using the `SAY: <MESSAGE>` command!


**Moving Objects**

* You can only move objects to cells within the bounds of the grid. Otherwise you will be penalized.
* Describe the position you want to move the object to using only the background as reference, and not the other objects!


**End of Game**

If you think you reached the goal of aligning all objects, you can ask the other player to finish the game by sending `SAY: finished?`. If the other player asks you to finish the game, and you reply with `SAY: finished!`, the game will end.

Both players win if the game ends within 24 rounds, where one round is defined as two players each sending a valid command.

**Scoring:**

The closer the identical objects are in both game boards, the more points you get. Penalties reduce your points. Can you beat the record?
